coredata.dc:description: A straightforward and fast approach has been established for detecting iron ions (Fe3+) and L-cysteine (CYS) using fluorescent carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N,S-CDs), synthesized from ribavirin and thiourea as precursor materials. The resulting N,S-CDs exhibit strong water solubility, stable blue fluorescence, and a notable quantum yield of 23.7 %, with fluorescence properties independent of excitation wavelength. These N,S-CDs were further characterized via FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS analyses to evaluate their surface functionalities, structural features, and elemental composition. Morphological analysis reveals spherical particles averaging 3.2 ± 0.2 nm in diameter, decorated with hydroxyl, carbonyl, thiol, and amino functional groups on the surface. The N,S-CDs displayed highly selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching (turn-off) in response to Fe3+ ions, with a detection threshold of 187 nM and a linear response range of 0–50 µM. Remarkably, the quenched N,S-CDs/Fe3+ fluorescence was reactivated by the addition of CYS, enabling CYS detection at a limit of 87.1 nM and within a linear range of 0–100 µM. The fluorescence switching between off and on states was consistently achievable for over 10 cycles by alternately adding Fe3+ and CYS, demonstrating excellent reversibility. The high selectivity and sensitivity of the N,S-CDs enabled their application as “on-off-on” fluorescent switches, successfully used for Fe3+ detection in real water samples, supporting environmental monitoring and safety. Additionally, cytotoxicity tests indicate low toxicity, allowing for successful use in intracellular imaging of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ and N,S-CDs/Fe3+ with CYS in HCT-116 cells. Thus, these fluorescent N,S-CDs represent a promising sensing platform for label-free detection of metal ions and biomolecules.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Vellore Institute of Technology deserves our sincere appreciation for their invaluable contributions to our research, particularly in providing laboratory and instrumental support. We also extend our gratitude to DST-VIT-FIST for their support.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: He received a doctoral degree from Alagappa University and has 10 years of research experience in Materials Chemistry, Supramolecular Chemistry, Organic Synthesis for Chemical Sensors, Computational Chemistry & Bioimaging Applications. He is the recipient of several awards including Basic Scientific Research-Junior Research Fellow (BSR-JRF) and extended Senior Research Fellow (BSR-SRF), University Grant Commission (UGC), India (2011\u20132015). He received the award (Ministry of Science and Technology) MOST Fellowship for Post-Doctoral Fellow at the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan (2017\u20132018). He worked as a Post-Doctoral Researcher in the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea (2018\u20132020). Currently, he is working as a Research Professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. His research interests are focused on organic materials and small molecules-derived carbon nanomaterials for Chemosensor, environmental, and live cell imaging.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A straightforward and fast approach has been established for detecting iron ions (Fe3+) and L-cysteine (CYS) using fluorescent carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N,S-CDs), synthesized from ribavirin and thiourea as precursor materials. The resulting N,S-CDs exhibit strong water solubility, stable blue fluorescence, and a notable quantum yield of 23.7 %, with fluorescence properties independent of excitation wavelength. These N,S-CDs were further characterized via FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS analyses to evaluate their surface functionalities, structural features, and elemental composition. Morphological analysis reveals spherical particles averaging 3.2 ± 0.2 nm in diameter, decorated with hydroxyl, carbonyl, thiol, and amino functional groups on the surface. The N,S-CDs displayed highly selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching (turn-off) in response to Fe3+ ions, with a detection threshold of 187 nM and a linear response range of 0–50 µM. Remarkably, the quenched N,S-CDs/Fe3+ fluorescence was reactivated by the addition of CYS, enabling CYS detection at a limit of 87.1 nM and within a linear range of 0–100 µM. The fluorescence switching between off and on states was consistently achievable for over 10 cycles by alternately adding Fe3+ and CYS, demonstrating excellent reversibility. The high selectivity and sensitivity of the N,S-CDs enabled their application as “on-off-on” fluorescent switches, successfully used for Fe3+ detection in real water samples, supporting environmental monitoring and safety. Additionally, cytotoxicity tests indicate low toxicity, allowing for successful use in intracellular imaging of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ and N,S-CDs/Fe3+ with CYS in HCT-116 cells. Thus, these fluorescent N,S-CDs represent a promising sensing platform for label-free detection of metal ions and biomolecules.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as versatile nanomaterials with significant potential across diverse applications, particularly in the field of sensing [1]. CDs exhibit unique properties, such as high sensitivity, excellent photostability, and fast response to environmental changes, making them highly effective for detecting a range of analytes, including metal ions, pH, glucose, and various biomolecules [2]. Their role in clinical diagnostics and environmental monitoring is increasingly well-recognized, as CDs can rapidly and accurately signal the presence of specific substances. Moreover, CDs have demonstrated efficacy as nanocarriers in targeted drug delivery, where their surfaces can be chemically modified to enhance compatibility with therapeutic agents or to enable selective binding to cellular targets [3]. These properties facilitate controlled and efficient drug release, positioning CDs as promising tools in modern therapeutics. Beyond medical and environmental sensing, CDs find widespread use in advanced technologies. Their applications including sensors, photocatalysis, and optoelectronics, as well as antibacterial and antiviral agents, due to their ability to interact with biological entities in various contexts [4]. Among CDs, heteroatom-doped carbon dots, particularly those doped with elements like nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S), have attracted substantial research interest due to the enhanced functional properties imparted by the heteroatoms [5,6]. The introduction of heteroatoms into the carbon matrix can significantly influence the electronic structure and chemical reactivity of CDs, making them more effective in various applications, including sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, and drug delivery [5–7]. N,S-CDs exhibit particularly advantageous characteristics. Nitrogen atoms, when introduced into the carbon framework, enhance electron-donating capabilities and improve fluorescence quantum yields [8]. Sulfur doping further enhances these effects by introducing redox-active centers and providing additional functional groups for interaction with analytes [9,10].

Recently, N,S-CDs have been studied as effective fluorescent chemosensors for the detection of metal ions and biological molecules [11–17]. The development of fluorescent chemosensors for metal ions, especially those integral to biological processes such as osmotic regulation, enzymatic catalysis, metabolic pathways, bio-mineralization, and cellular signaling, has attracted notable attention [18,19]. Iron (Fe3+) ions, for instance, are essential across all living organisms due to their fundamental role in metabolic processes [20]. However, imbalances in iron levels can lead to various health conditions [21]. In humans, iron deficiency is associated with anemia, impaired organ function, and increased susceptibility to diseases like diabetes and cardiovascular ailments [22]. Conversely, excessive iron accumulation is linked to a higher risk of cancers and Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s, including neurodegenerative diseases [23]. Elevated iron concentrations also pose ecological risks, underscoring the need for effective and selective Fe3+ ions chemosensors in environmental monitoring and biomedical diagnostics [24]. To date, extensive research has been conducted to investigate the efficient detection of Fe3+ ions using CDs as a fluorescent material. For example, Y. Wang et al. prepared the concentration-dependent fluorescence properties of N-CQDs, influenced by aggregation and sp2 domain changes, enable their application as sensitive probes for Fe3+ ions detection [25]. Q. Wang et al. synthesized Tangshai-derived carbon dots (TSCD) exhibit unique fluorescence properties enabling highly selective and sensitive Fe3+ ions detection, facilitated by their superior coordination constant and dual-color fluorescence ratiometric sensing [26]. B. Li et al. reported the lanthanum and cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots (LaCe-CQDs) possess stable fluorescence properties and high selectivity, making them a promising sensor for Fe3+ ions detection in diverse environmental samples [27]. H.S. George et al. prepared the papaya seed-derived carbon dots, synthesized via a simple microwave-assisted method, exhibit strong fluorescence quenching and high sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions, making them effective eco-friendly sensors for heavy metal detection [28]. Y. He et al. synthesized the green tea residue-derived carbon dots exhibit strong fluorescence quenching with Fe3+ ions, enabling sensitive detection and practical applications in environmental monitoring and biological imaging [29]. K. Annamalai et al. reported the nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) exhibit excitation-dependent luminescence and are highly sensitive for detecting Fe3+ ions and Picric acid through inner-filter effect (IFE) and FRET, with potential applications in environmental sensing and biological systems [30]. These studies highlight CDs potential for enhancing the precision and reliability of Fe3+ ions detection, which is crucial in environmental monitoring, biomedical applications, and industrial processes. By leveraging the advanced photophysical and chemical properties of CDs, researchers have developed innovative approaches to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of Fe3+ ions detection systems. Although significant progress has been made in the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs), further advancements are necessary to develop fluorescent probe materials with enhanced photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) and improved sensitivity for Fe3+ ion detection. Achieving these qualities is critical for expanding the applicability and efficiency of CDs in sensing applications. N,S-CDs have shown promising results in selectively detecting Fe3+ ions, based on mechanisms like fluorescence quenching, which occurs through interactions between Fe3+ ions and the functional groups present on N,S-CDs surfaces. In addition to Fe3+ ions detection, N,S-CDs have been explored for sensing biomolecules such as L-cysteine.

L-cysteine (CYS) is another critical biomolecule that stands out among amino acids due to its unique thiol (−SH) group, which endows it with distinctive biochemical properties [31]. Present in diverse biological systems, CYS is essential for protein synthesis, cellular metabolism, and antioxidant defense mechanisms [32]. The presence of functional groups like amine, carboxyl, and thiol on N,S-CDs enables specific interactions with CYS, making them suitable for selective and sensitive detection. Furthermore, the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ ions and CYS using a single sensor system is of particular interest, as it offers an integrated approach to monitor both metal ion and amino acid levels in complex environments [33–37]. Conventional detection methods for Fe3+ ions and CYS, like chromatographic techniques, are highly accurate but often involve complex, time-intensive procedures and require costly equipment [38,39]. This underscores the demand for a more accessible, rapid, and cost-effective detection method that is also sensitive and selective [33–37]. Despite these advancements, this study introduces a novel design of carbon dots (CDs) featuring a unique and highly sensitive “turn-off-on” fluorescence mode tailored for the simultaneous and selective detection of Fe3+ ions and CYS. By optimizing N,S-doped CDs, the research addresses the challenge of enhancing sensitivity, selectivity, and quantum yield (QY), making them more effective for environmental and biomedical applications. The proposed dual-detection system provides a versatile, cost-effective solution for sensing both metal ions and biomolecules, offering significant advancements over traditional single-analyte sensors. Furthermore, the study emphasizes improving the biocompatibility and reducing the toxicity of N,S-CDs, paving the way for their integration into safer, more reliable sensing technologies.

In this study, we introduce a novel design of carbon dots (CDs) featuring a unique and highly sensitive “turn-off-on” fluorescence mode tailored for the efficient and accurate detection of Fe3+ ions and CYS (Scheme 1 ). The N,S-CDs were produced through a single-step hydrothermal method using ribavirin and thiourea as precursor materials. The synthesized N,S-CDs underwent comprehensive characterization using FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS analyses to investigate their surface functionalities, structural properties, and elemental composition. Morphological assessment indicated that the spherical particles with an average diameter of approximately 3.2 ± 0.2 nm. These N,S-CDs serve as effective “turn-off” fluorescent probes for Fe3+ ions, demonstrating excellent selectivity, low detection limits, and a wide linear detection range. Notably, the quenched fluorescence signal is restored, or “turned on,” upon CYS addition, enabling highly selective and sensitive detection of CYS in real samples. Cytotoxicity assays demonstrated low toxicity of the N,S-CDs, enabling their effective use for intracellular imaging of both N,S-CDs/Fe3+ complexes and N,S-CDs/Fe3+ with CYS in HCT-116 cells. Consequently, these fluorescent N,S-CDs show strong potential as a versatile, label-free sensing platform for detecting metal ions and biomolecules.

Ribavirin and thiourea were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and all metal nitrates/chloride salts and amino acids were obtained from Merck. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used directly in the synthesis without additional purification. Milli-Q water was used to prepare all solutions throughout the experiment, ensuring high purity and consistency in the results.

The hydrothermal synthesis method is widely employed to prepare N,S-CDs. In a standard procedure, 0.618 g of ribavirin and 0.761 g of thiourea are dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, resulting in a uniform solution that is stirred magnetically for 30 min. The mixture is subsequently transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, where it is heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Once the reaction is complete and the mixture has cooled to room temperature, it undergoes centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 3 h. The resulting supernatant is filtered and freeze-dried to obtain the N,S-CDs, which are subsequently analyzed using various techniques such as UV–Vis, fluorescence, FT-IR, XRD, XPS, and TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity of N,S-CDs towards Fe3+ and CYS
ce:section.ce:para: In the fluorescence on–off-on sensing experiments, an appropriate concentration of N,S-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) was dissolved in 3.0 mL of HEPES buffer at pH 7.4 (10 mM). To evaluate the selectivity of N,S-CDs for Fe3+, 25 μL aliquots of various metal ion solutions (50 × 10−6 M) were added to 5 μL of N,S-CDs solution, followed by the addition of HEPES buffer to adjust the total volume to 2.0 mL at pH 7.4. For the turn-off sensing, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions, reaching up to 50 μM, were incrementally introduced to the N,S-CDs solution to diminish their fluorescence. To detect CYS, a final concentration of 50 μM Fe3+ was mixed with 2.0 mL of N,S-CDs (0.5 mg/mL), resulting in a solution termed N,S-CDs/Fe3+. Subsequently, varying amounts of CYS (100 × 10−6 M) were added to the N,S-CDs/Fe3+ mixture, resulting in final CYS concentrations (0 to 100 μM). Each sample was incubated at room temperature for 30 min, and fluorescence spectra were measured using excitation and emission wavelengths of 310 nm and 456 nm, respectively.

Additionally, two water samples were collected to evaluate the effectiveness of N,S-CDs in detecting Fe3+ in actual environmental conditions. The samples included tap water from our laboratory and lake water sourced from Yeungnam University campus. Various concentrations of Fe3+ (10, 30, and 50 μM) were introduced into the water samples for detection, with excitation set at 310 nm. All water samples were allowed to settle naturally for 24 h and then centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. After centrifugation, the supernatant was filtered through a cellulose acetate membrane filter with a 0.45 μm pore size prior to analysis.

Fig. 1 a presents the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs, highlighting key aspects of their electronic structure. A prominent peak at 270 nm indicates the π-π* transition within the sp2-hybridized carbon domains typical of graphitic structures [40]. The introduction of nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) dopants creates surface defects in the carbon dot lattice, resulting in new energy levels that enhance absorption into the visible spectrum [40]. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, N,S-CDs demonstrate maximum excitation/emission peaks at 310/456 nm with the narrow emission peak at 456 nm corresponding to blue fluorescence [41]. Moreover, the N,S-CDs dispersed in water show excitation-independent emission as the excitation wavelength varies from 250 to 380 nm (Fig. 1c).

This behavior indicates a consistent size distribution and surface state among the N,S-CDs, with the highest excitation intensity observed at 310 nm. This excitation-independent emission, a characteristic of carbon dots, is influenced by particle size distribution and surface state variations from N and S doping [42]. The quantum yield (QY) of N,S-CDs under 310 nm excitation is measured at 23.7 %, reflecting a high efficiency of light conversion into emitted FL. Such a QY indicates strong fluorescence and enhances their applicability in optoelectronic devices, biosensors, and imaging probes. The observed excitation-independent behavior arises from variations in particle size and surface state distribution, where differing sizes create a range of energy gaps for light absorption and emission. The dopants also introduce surface defects that modify electronic states, contributing to the tunable optical properties of N,S-CDs, making them suitable for diverse photonic and biomedical applications.

To achieve optimal stability, experimental settings such as pH and ionic strength were carefully optimized [43]. Fig. 2 a shows the stability of N,S-CDs evaluated across a broad pH range (3.0–12.0). Interestingly, FL intensity increased significantly as pH rose from 3.0 to 7.0 but decreased when pH was further raised from 8.0 to 12.0. As the pH increased from 3 to 9, the N,S-CDs exhibited a gradual shift toward more negative surface charges, driven by surface charge modifications from protonation and deprotonation processes [44]. Additionally, the aqueous N,S-CDs solution showed excellent salt resistance, indicating its potential for use in various environments. The effect of electrolyte concentration on N,S-CDs FL properties was also examined. N,S-CDs solutions were prepared with NaCl (0 to 1.0 M) to assess their stability in different ionic strengths (Fig. 2b). The FL intensity of N,S-CDs remained stable across varying NaCl concentrations, demonstrating their robustness against fluctuations in ionic strength. This consistency in performance is advantageous for potential applications. Even after 30 min of radiation exposure, the FL intensity of N,S-CDs decreased slightly, retaining about 94 % of its initial intensity [45] (Fig. 2c). This resilience indicates that N,S-CDs are highly resistant to radiation-induced chemical or physical changes, making them suitable for environments where radiation exposure is a concern. Similarly, the FL intensity showed minimal decline after 60 days of storage (Fig. 2d), reflecting their long-term stability and indicating minimal degradation over time. This durability is essential for practical applications that require reliable performance, such as in sensor devices and imaging systems. Overall, these findings highlight the exceptional stability of N,S-CDs, reinforcing their suitability for fluorescence sensing and imaging. Their ability to maintain optical properties under prolonged radiation exposure and extended storage enhances their utility in diverse environments. Additionally, the favourable optical characteristics and stability of N,S-CDs position them as promising candidates for biomedical applications, where reliability and consistency are critical.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological features of N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural characteristics of N,S-CDs were thoroughly analyzed using advanced characterization techniques, including FTIR, XRD, HR-TEM, and XPS analyses. FTIR spectra of N,S-CDs (Fig. 3 a), the absorption band around 3362 cm−1 indicates O–H stretching vibrations, signifying the presence of hydroxyl groups [46]. The peak at 3010 cm−1 corresponds to N–H stretching, confirming the combination of N into the CDs [47]. Peaks at 2949 cm−1 are attributed to C–H stretching, indicating the occurrence of alkyl chains. A distinct peak at 2649 cm−1 is assigned to S–H stretching, demonstrating successful sulfur incorporation into the CDs. Further peaks at 1714 cm−1 and 1449 cm−1 correspond to C = O and C = N stretching vibrations, respectively, confirming the presence of carbonyl and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface. Additionally, peaks at 1520 cm−1 and 1213 cm−1 indicate C–C and C–S stretching in aromatic rings, suggesting benzene-like structures within the CDs. Overall, these results reveal the complex surface chemistry of N,S-CDs, highlighting various functional groups such as O–H, C = N, C–S, C = O, and S–H.

The crystalline structure and purity of the synthesized N,S-CDs were analyzed using XRD pattern, as illustrated in Fig. 3b. The XRD pattern for N,S-CDs displays a prominent peak at 2θ = 21.3°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. This peak suggests the presence of an amorphous carbon phase along with graphitic carbon characteristics [48]. TEM analysis was employed to accurately investigate the particle size and surface morphology of N,S-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 3c. The analysis revealed that the N,S-CDs are evenly dispersed in an aqueous solution and display a spherical shape [49], with diameters ranging from 1 to 6 nm (Fig. 3c and d). Notably, a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was identified in the inset of Fig. 3c, which corresponds to the in-plane lattice spacing characteristic of graphitic (sp2) carbon. This observation confirms the existence of graphitic domains within the N,S-CDs, suggesting enhanced electronic properties due to their graphitic nature. Furthermore, a statistical evaluation of 100 individual particles revealed an average diameter of 3.2 ± 0.2 nm, indicating a narrow size distribution and a well-defined nanostructure.

The XPS analysis provided robust evidence for the elemental composition and surface functional groups of the synthesized N,S-CDs. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 a) reveals four primary peaks at of 400.3, 162.3, 285.1, and 532.0 eV binding energies, consistent to the elements N1s, S2p, C1s, and O1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, the N1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three main peaks, which match to distinct nitrogen-containing functional groups: C = N–C at 398.2 eV, N–C at 399.5 eV, and C = O/C = N at 401.4 eV. This suggests a diverse nitrogen bonding environment within the structure of the N,S-CDs [50]. Further details of sulfur incorporation were identified in the high-resolution S2p spectrum (Fig. 4c), which shows a broad peak at lower binding energies. This peak comprises three distinct sub-peaks, including those at 162.7 eV and 164.4 eV, which are associated with C–S (S2p3/2) and C–S (S2p1/2) bonds, respectively, confirming successful sulfur doping within the N,S-CD [51]. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) reveals four distinct peaks at 282.8, 284.2, 285.8, 287.1 and 288.1 eV, conforming to different types of carbon bonding environments: C–C, C = C, C–N/C–S, C–O, and C = O, respectively. Fig. 4e displays two peaks in the O1s spectrum at 531.1 and 532.0 eV, which correspond to the C = O and C-O-C/C-OH functional groups [52], respectively. The surface composition identified through XPS aligns with results obtained from FT-IR analysis, indicating that nitrogen and sulfur are co-doped within the N,S-CDs. Together, the FT-IR and XPS findings confirm the presence of various functional groups, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, thiol, and amino groups, on the N,S-CD surfaces.

To assess the effectiveness and selectivity of N,S-CDs as a fluorescence-based sensor for Fe3+ ions, the FL response of N,S-CDs was measured in the presence of various potentially interfering metal ions under controlled conditions. The results indicated that, even when other metal ions were present at concentrations 10 times higher than that of Fe3+, the FL intensity of the N,S-CD solution remained mostly unaffected [33–37]. This was demonstrated in Fig. 5 a, which reveals a stable FL response even with a 100-fold excess of other metals. Fig. 5b, a bar graph comparing fluorescence intensities, confirms that different metal ions minimally affect the FL of N,S-CDs in aqueous solutions. Additionally, the color change of N,S-CDs, observed under both daylight and UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 5c), highlights an “on-off” effect, further emphasizing the strong selectivity of N,S-CDs for Fe3+ detection. The minimal interference from other substances confirms that N,S-CDs are highly selective for Fe3+ ions, making them a reliable sensor for Fe3+ detection.

Sensitivity is a vital parameter for evaluating the performance of fluorescent sensors like N,S-CDs. To assess this, FL titration experiments were carried out by adding varying Fe3+ concentrations to a fixed amount of N,S-CDs. Fig. 6 a shows that N,S-CDs have a characteristic FL emission peak at 456 nm when excited at 310 nm in an aqueous solution [53]. When Fe3+ is added, FL intensity decreases proportionally with Fe3+ concentration, likely due to the binding of Fe3+ to the N,S-CD surface [54], as illustrated in Fig. 6b. Fig. 6c presents a steady decrease in FL intensity with increased Fe3+ concentrations, eventually reaching a saturation point at around 50 μM. This effect is thought to result from interactions between Fe3+ and –OH groups on the N,S-CD surface, promoting electron transfer that diminishes fluorescence. The relationship between FL intensity (F0/F) and Fe3+ over the 0–50 μM concentration range is linear, described by the equation (F0/F) = 0.0557 x + 0.8931, with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9888). Using the formula DetectionLimit = K × SD/S, (where K = 3 signal–noise-ration, SD is the standard deviation, and S is the slope) [55–57], the detection limit for Fe3+ was determined to be 187 nM, indicating strong sensitivity for Fe3+ detection.

Beyond Fe3+, the N,S-CDs/Fe3+ system was tested for its effectiveness in detecting CYS, a sulfur-containing amino acid, by measuring fluorescence intensity changes as CYS concentrations increased. When CYS was added to the N,S-CDs/Fe3+ system, the FL intensity was restored progressively, suggesting that CYS effectively competes with Fe3+ for binding sites on N,S-CDs, thereby restoring fluorescence [33–37]. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, FL recovery was directly proportional to the concentration of CYS (up to 100 μM). Fig. 7c shows a consistent increase in fluorescence intensity with increasing CYS concentrations, and Fig. 7d depicts a linear relationship between FL intensity and CYS across the 0–100 µM concentration range, with the calibration curve described by the equation 1/[F − F0] = 2.3950x + 0.0052, where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities with and without CYS, respectively. The detection limit for CYS was calculated to be 87.1 nM, as illustrated in Fig. 7e. Additionally, the time-dependent fluorescence response (Fig. 7f) revealed that fluorescence quenching due to Fe3+ occurred rapidly, within 3 s, followed by a restoration of fluorescence at 420 nm upon the addition of CYS. These results demonstrate the quick response capability of N,S-CDs, making them suitable for real-time detection of Fe3+ and CYS.

The N,S-CD were evaluated as fluorescence sensors for the detection of Fe3+ and CYS, and their performance was compared with that of other sensors, as summarized in Table S1. Based on the data presented in Table S1, the N,S-CD exhibit significant potential as a dual-mode probe for detecting Fe3+ and CYS using an “on-off-on” fluorescence mechanism. In this detection strategy, the presence of Fe3+ initially quenches the fluorescence signal (“off” state) of the N,S-CD due to specific interactions or electron transfer effects. When CYS is introduced, the fluorescence signal is restored (“on” state) because CYS strongly interacts with Fe3+, forming a stable complex and effectively displacing it from the N,S-CD surface. This fluorescence recovery demonstrates the specificity and sensitivity of the N,S-CD for both analytes, making them a versatile and effective tool for environmental monitoring and biochemical analysis.

Reversible sensing behavior is critical for practical applications and for understanding sensor mechanisms. Previous studies have shown limited reversibility in Fe3+-responsive systems, but this study demonstrated that the N,S-CDs system maintains “on-off-on” reversibility across multiple cycles. By sequentially adding Fe3+ (50 μM) and CYS (100 μM) to the N,S-CDs solution, it was observed that fluorescence recovery and quenching efficiencies were maintained up to 90 % after 10 cycles (Fig. 8 a) [33]. Fig. 8b further illustrates this cycle, showing high reversibility, with initial Fe3+ and CYS concentrations of 50 μM and 100 μM, respectively, in the first cycle. Based on these results, N,S-CDs offer a sensitive, selective, and reversible FL-based sensor system for the detection of Fe3+ and CYS. The quick response and reliable selectivity make this system well-suited for applications requiring fast and precise monitoring of Fe3+ and CYS, with minimal interference from other substances.

These results underscore the high sensitivity of N,S-CDs for detecting Fe3+ ions, confirming their potential as an analytical tool for environmental monitoring. To further evaluate their practical application, the N,S-CDs were tested for Fe3+ detection in tap water and lake water samples, with the analytical outcomes summarized in Table 1 . In this experiment, N,S-CDs solutions were combined with Fe3+ ions at concentrations of 10, 30, and 50 μM, and the fluorescence intensities were recorded accordingly. The recovery rates of Fe3+ ions in real water samples ranged from 98.6 % to 103.5 %, indicating reliable accuracy, while the relative detection sensitivity (RDS%) was low, varying between 1.37 % and 3.18 % [13,16]. These findings highlight the N,S-CDs robustness and accuracy in detecting Fe3+ across different environmental conditions, suggesting their potential for widespread use in monitoring Fe3+ contamination in water sources.

To investigate the potential of N,S-CDs for bioimaging and their suitability in biological applications, a cell viability assay and cellular imaging were conducted using human colorectal carcinoma cells (HCT-116) [58]. The WST assay was used to assess cytotoxicity and sensor viability for detecting Fe3+ and CYS. HCT-116 cells were exposed to various N,S-CDs concentrations (0–150 μg/mL) over a 24-hour period (Fig. 9 a). Results showed that the N,S-CDs showed minimal cytotoxicity, with cell viability exceeding 94 % across all tested concentrations. This high cell viability underscores the biocompatibility of N,S-CDs and their suitability for potential bioimaging applications, indicating that they are safe for use in cellular environments [59–61]. To assess the feasibility of utilizing N,S-CDs for cellular imaging, HCT-116 cells were incubated with 150 μg/mL of N,S-CDs at 37 °C for 15 min.

Following incubation, the cells were washed with PBS to eliminate any unbound N,S-CDs and then imaged using CLSM. In live cells lacking Fe3+, the N,S-CDs presented strong intrinsic FL (Fig. 9b), indicating effective uptake by the cells. However, upon introducing Fe3+ to the cells, FL quenching was observed, suggesting the formation of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ complexes within the cellular environment [62,63]. The reversibility of the N,S-CDs sensing system was additional established by introducing CYS to the N,S-CDs/Fe3+ complex-loaded cells. Upon CYS addition, FL was restored, demonstrating the “on-off-on” response of the N,S-CDs in a biological setting (Fig. 9b). Phase-contrast overlay images confirmed the intracellular localization of N,S-CDs, suggesting that these carbon dots can penetrate cell membranes efficiently. These results indicate that N,S-CDs possess strong potential as fluorescence-based sensors for bioimaging applications, enabling effective and reversible detection of Fe3+ and CYS in biological systems. This reversible fluorescence response, combined with low cytotoxicity and high cellular uptake, makes N,S-CDs a promising tool for monitoring Fe3+ and CYS levels in live cell imaging and other biomedical applications.

A straightforward, cost-effective, and efficient one-pot hydrothermal synthesis method was utilized to create nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using ribavirin and thiourea as precursors. The synthesized N,S-CDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence properties, achieving a high quantum yield (QY) of up to 23.7 %. These properties enable the N,S-CDs to serve as selective and sensitive FL sensors for “off–on” detection of Fe3+ and CYS. The detection mechanism is based on a fluorescence “turn-off” and “turn-on” response. In the presence of Fe3+, fluorescence is completely quenched due to static quenching, resulting in a stable, non-fluorescent N,S-CDs/Fe3+ complex. This “turn-off” effect is reversed when CYS is introduced, which breaks the N,S-CDs/Fe3+ complex. The CYS then binds to Fe3+ through an Fe3+-S bond, creating a more stable Fe3+–CYS complex, thereby reactivating fluorescence in a “turn-on” mode. Selectivity studies confirmed that the N,S-CDs specifically responds to Fe3+ and CYS, even in the occurrence of other possibly interfering metal ions and thiol-containing compounds. Sensitivity was further confirmed by detection limits of 187 nM in the 0 − 50 μM range for Fe3+ and 87.1 nM in the 0 − 100 μM range for CYS. The N,S-CD sensor platform was effectively applied for detecting Fe3+ ions in real water samples, yielding promising results. Notably, the platform demonstrated a “switch-on” fluorescence response when used in cellular imaging by interacting with Fe3+ ions and CYS, enabling the potential for real-time in vivo monitoring. This capability for fluorescence activation upon binding with Fe3+ and CYS not only confirms the sensor high selectivity but also underscores its utility in live biological settings, paving the way for applications in real-time bioimaging and diagnostics.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ramamoorthi Tamizhselvi: She received her MSc degree in 2017 from Thiruvalluvar University, India, and her PhD in 2024 from Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, India. Her research focuses on the design and synthesis of novel Schiff base molecules for applications as sensors, including the selective detection of metal ions, real-sample analysis, and biological studies.

Kuppu Sakthi Velu: He completed his Ph.D. in 2021 under the guidance of Dr. T. Stalin at the School of Chemical Sciences, Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi-03, Tamil Nadu, and India. He then worked as a postdoctoral fellow at SSN College of Engineering, Anna University, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India (2021-2022), and subsequently at Yeungnam University, South Korea (2022-2023). Currently, he serves as an International Research Professor (Assistant Professor) in the School of Chemical Engineering at Yeungnam University, South Korea. His research focuses on MXene-based heterostructured materials for applications in energy conversion, energy storage, and biomedical fields.

Ayyakannu Arumugam Napoleon: Dr. A.A.Napoleon is an avid researcher and Professor at VIT University, Vellore. He has completed his Ph.D. in 2015 under the guidance of Dr. F. Nawaz Khan, from Department of Chemistry, VIT University, Vellore on the topic of “Studies on synthesis, biological evaluations of few nitrogen and oxygen heterocycles (pyrazolines, isochromen-1- ones, xanthenediones). He has completed his master degree M.Pharm from Birla Institute of Technology in 1997 and Bachelor degree B.Pharm from The Tamilnadu Dr MGR Medical University, Chennai, India in 1994. He has published over 55 Research papers with good impact factor journal publications. His current research work is primarily focuses on the development of probe as Chemosensor for metal ion sensing applications, environmental, live cell imaging, and developing Carbon Nanomaterials. Also designing novel heterocyclic compounds and exploring for suitable pharmacological applications from synthetic medicinal chemistry and from natural products.

Muhammad Saeed Akhtar: Muhammad Saeed Akhtar, from Havelian, Pakistan, earned his Ph.D. in 2020 under the supervision of Prof. Yong Rok Lee at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea. After completing his Ph.D., he worked as a postdoctoral fellow at the same institution from 2020 to 2021. He currently holds the position of International Research Professor at the Department of Chemistry, Yeungnam University. His research focuses on transition metal-catalyzed C–H functionalization, annulation reactions, and organocatalyzed synthesis of diverse aromatic compounds.

Naushad Ahmad: Dr. Ahmad did his Ph.D from the prestigious Central University, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi India in 2009 on the topic of “Synthesis of bridged bimetallic complexes: Charact erization and physico-chemical studies”. He has completed his master and Bachelor degree from the Aligarh Muslim Univ ersity, Aligarh, U.P. India. His current Research interest is: Chemical sensors, Biosensors, Supercapacitors, H2 production, petroleum refining, Battery, Perovskites, Nanomaterials, Ferrites, Metal oxides, Graphene and heterogeneous systems. He has published more than 150 research articles in reputed International SCI Journals. Current Research interest: Synthesis of nanostructured materials (metal, metal oxides, carbon nanotubes, and graphene) and their applications as a sensor, supercapacitor, and in conversion reactions (DRM, SRM and alcohols).

Subramanian Palanisamy: He is a working as a Research Professor at the East Coast Life Sciences Institute, Gangneung-Wonju National University, Republic of Korea. He completed his M.Sc and Ph.D. in the Department of Animal Health and Management, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, Tamil Nadu, India. During his doctoral and pre-doctoral research, he worked on polysaccharides, nanomaterials, and their biological activities. His current research interests include the biological activities of natural polysaccharides, the development of polysaccharides conjugates for drug delivery, and the synthesis of green nanomaterials for biological applications.

SangGuan You: He is currently a Professor in the Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, College of Life Science, Republic of Korea. He earned his Bachelor’s and Master’s degrees from Gangneung National University and Korea University in 1997 and 1999, respectively. He completed his Doctorate in Food Engineering, University of Manitoba, Canada, and later worked as a Senior Researcher at the University of Manitoba (2005). He is a member of the American Association of Cereal Chemist and serves as an editorial board member of Bioactive Carbohydrates and Dietary Fiber. His current research focuses on the structural analysis of polysaccharides, the preparation of polysaccharide conjugates for drug delivery, and their applications in pharmaceutical activities, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anticancer, and immunostimulatory properties.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: He received a doctoral degree from Alagappa University and has 10 years of research experience in Materials Chemistry, Supramolecular Chemistry, Organic Synthesis for Chemical Sensors, Computational Chemistry & Bioimaging Applications. He is the recipient of several awards including Basic Scientific Research-Junior Research Fellow (BSR-JRF) and extended Senior Research Fellow (BSR-SRF), University Grant Commission (UGC), India (2011–2015). He received the award (Ministry of Science and Technology) MOST Fellowship for Post-Doctoral Fellow at the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan (2017–2018). He worked as a Post-Doctoral Researcher in the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea (2018–2020). Currently, he is working as a Research Professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. His research interests are focused on organic materials and small molecules-derived carbon nanomaterials for Chemosensor, environmental, and live cell imaging.

Seong-Cheol Kim: Prof. Seong-Cheol Kim is a faculty member at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea, specializing in Polymer Science and Engineering. He earned his Ph.D. in Polymer Science & Engineering from the University of Massachusetts Lowell (2001–2006) and an M.Eng. in Chemical Engineering from Seoul National University (1998–2000). His research focuses on advanced materials, including polymers, nanomaterials, and biomaterials, with applications in energy systems and renewable technologies. At Yeungnam University, he is part of a dynamic team advancing innovation through government-backed initiatives like the Brain Korea 21st Century programs, aimed at fostering emerging technologies in energy systems and green materials​.
